comments:
Summary
This paper proposes Neural Combinatorial Optimization, a framework that trains neural sequence models (pointer networks) with policy-gradient reinforcement learning to solve combinatorial optimization problems. The work applies an actor-critic training scheme to learn a stochastic policy over tours for Euclidean TSP instances, and proposes two inference/search strategies: (i) sampling many candidate tours from a trained policy (with temperature tuning) and (ii) Active Search, which adapts model parameters on a single test instance via policy gradients. The paper evaluates variants (RL pretraining with greedy/sampling/active search, pure Active Search) on TSP20/50/100 (uniform random points) and shows improved performance over supervised pointer networks and Christofides, approaching optimality in many cases. The method is also demonstrated on knapsack instances.

Strengths
- Clear motivation: supervised pointer training requires high-quality labels and does not necessarily generalize; RL is a natural alternative when only solution quality (reward) is available. The paper argues this convincingly.
- Reasonable and well-explained methodology: use of pointer networks, REINFORCE with a learned critic, and practical tricks (glimpses, temperature, logit clipping) are clearly described and justified.
- Multiple inference/search modes are studied, including a neat Active Search idea that optimizes on a single test instance.
- Empirical results show that RL training improves over supervised pointer networks and improves over a simple guaranteed heuristic (Christofides); sampling/active-search combine to reach near-optimal tours on small-to-medium instances.
- The paper discusses generalization to other problems and shows initial results on knapsack, indicating flexibility.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Scalability and computational cost: the reported strong results use very large numbers of samples per instance (e.g. 1,280,000 samples) or long Active Search runs (hours per instance for larger problems). This is computationally expensive compared to classical solvers; the paper remarks on speed limitations but more explicit per-instance compute/energy comparisons and sample-efficiency analyses are needed to judge practicality.
- Comparison fairness/clarity: several baselines (OR-Tools, Concorde, LK-H) are run on CPUs while the learning methods are run on a GPU. More thorough wall-clock comparisons that control for hardware and parallelization would help readers evaluate trade-offs between solution quality and runtime.
- Limited problem distributions and sizes: experiments focus on uniformly random planar points up to 100 cities. It is unclear how well the learned policies generalize to other point distributions (clustered, real-world) or scale to larger instances beyond 100. The paper acknowledges these limits but empirical evidence would strengthen claims about generality.
- Active Search behavior: the paper notes that Active Search sometimes converges to suboptimal regions when starting from a pretrained model (TSP50). A deeper analysis/diagnosis (e.g., visualization of the optimization trajectory, effect of learning rate and baseline choices) would be useful to understand failure modes and robustness.
- Feasibility constraints and broader applicability: while the paper discusses handling infeasible branches (penalization vs masking), no concrete experiments explore constrained variants (e.g., TSP with time windows). More experimental support is needed to substantiate claims of general applicability.
- Ablations and sensitivity: the architecture and training include multiple design choices (glimpses, number of processing steps in critic, temperature, logit clipping). The paper would benefit from ablation studies quantifying the contribution of each component and sensitivity to hyperparameters.

Soundness
The presented algorithms and objective derivations are standard and sound (REINFORCE, actor-critic). The empirical methodology is reasonable and experimental details are provided for reproducibility (architecture sizes, batch sizes, learning rates decay, etc.). However, given the heavy reliance on massive sampling and long per-instance optimization at inference, it is important to be clear about computational budgets and to analyze sample efficiency; otherwise claims about being a generally useful tool are over-optimistic.

Originality
Applying policy-gradient RL to pointer-network-style models for combinatorial optimization and proposing Active Search on single instances is an interesting combination. While components (pointer networks, REINFORCE, actor-critic) are known, their integration and the empirical study on TSP and knapsack constitute a meaningful and original contribution.

Suggestions for improvement
1) Report per-instance wall-clock time and number of model forward/backward passes for each method (greedy, sampling, active search) and for each baseline under matched hardware conditions; include sample-efficiency curves.
2) Include ablations on the main architectural/training choices (glimpses, temperature, logit clipping, critic depth/processing steps) to show which elements matter most.
3) Evaluate generalization: test trained models on other point distributions (clustered, non-uniform) and on larger instances to assess robustness and scalability.
4) Analyze Active Search dynamics: when and why it moves towards suboptimal regions; study effects of learning rate, baseline form, and initialization.
5) For constrained problems, include at least one small empirical experiment (e.g., simple time-window constraints) showing how feasibility can be enforced or learned.
6) Be explicit about costs: if very large sampling budgets are required to match classical solvers, clarify practical scenarios where this approach is advantageous (e.g., where handcrafted heuristics are unavailable or when amortized inference matters).

Recommendation
I recommend acceptance. The paper presents an interesting and well-executed study that combines neural sequence models and reinforcement learning to tackle combinatorial optimization, demonstrates promising results on standard benchmarks, and opens useful directions (Active Search, applying RL to combinatorial solvers). The main limitations are computational cost and limited evaluation breadth; addressing these in follow-up work or in a revision would substantially strengthen the contribution.
